jokes.text: Where is my desk?
jokes.setup: Cold open with character's confused question about missing desk
jokes.punchline: The entire premise that someone's desk has vanished
jokes.explanation: Classic Office cold open setup - simple premise that immediately establishes the prank scenario. Strong character integration as this is peak Dwight confusion.

jokes.text: Okay, well, you're the one who lost the desk. / I didn't lose my desk
jokes.setup: Dwight's panicked search for his missing desk
jokes.punchline: Jim's casual implication that Dwight somehow 'lost' a desk
jokes.explanation: Jim's deadpan response to Dwight's crisis perfectly captures their dynamic. The absurdity of 'losing' a desk is classic Jim logic.

jokes.text: Where was the last place you saw it?
jokes.setup: Dwight desperately looking for his missing desk
jokes.punchline: Jim treating a desk like a lost set of keys
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the absurdity. Jim's helpful tone while giving advice for finding a massive piece of furniture is perfectly crafted character comedy.

jokes.text: I think you should retrace your steps.
jokes.setup: Continuing the 'lost desk' premise
jokes.punchline: Further treating the desk like a small lost object
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up that maintains the absurd logic. Jim's commitment to the bit is what makes it work.

jokes.text: Colder. Warmer. Little warmer...
jokes.setup: Dwight searching for his desk
jokes.punchline: Jim turning it into a hot/cold game like finding hidden treasure
jokes.explanation: Fantastic escalation that transforms the prank into an interactive game. The extended sequence of temperature cues is both cruel and hilarious.

jokes.text: Jim, I've given you this information like 20 times. / I know.
jokes.setup: Jim calling Dwight for basic pricing information
jokes.punchline: Jim's casual admission that he already knows but keeps asking anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim harassment of Dwight. The casual 'I know' reveals the pointless nature of the call, making Dwight's frustration funnier.

jokes.text: Wash your hands Kevin.
jokes.setup: Random workplace moment
jokes.punchline: Dwight's authoritative hygiene command to Kevin
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment that shows Dwight's bossy nature, but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: Hello, it's sempai. / Was that you mom? / No. That was my sensei. / Thought it was your mom.
jokes.setup: Dwight taking a formal karate call
jokes.punchline: Jim's implication that Dwight's sensei sounds like his mom
jokes.explanation: Good character-based humor that deflates Dwight's martial arts pretensions by suggesting his sensei sounds maternal.

jokes.text: Arigato goazai mashta. Hai.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to sound authentic on his karate call
jokes.punchline: His mangled pronunciation of Japanese phrases
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight trying too hard to be authentic. The cringe comes from his obvious mispronunciation while trying to sound expert.

jokes.text: I am now sempai, which is assistant sensei. / Assistant to the sensei, that's pretty cool. / Assistant sensei.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his karate promotion
jokes.punchline: Jim's deliberate misstatement echoing 'Assistant to the Regional Manager'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the series' running gag. Jim's correction perfectly parallels their workplace dynamic and Dwight's immediate defensive correction is perfect.

jokes.text: Not that a lot of people here in America know what a sempai is. But it's equally as respected as a sensei.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his karate rank in talking head
jokes.punchline: His condescending tone about American ignorance while clearly not understanding it himself
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dwight's pretentious ignorance. The claim that sempai is 'equally respected' reveals his lack of understanding.

jokes.text: Michael tends to procrastinate... a bit whenever he has to do work.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining Michael's work habits
jokes.punchline: The massive understatement of calling avoiding all work 'procrastinating a bit'
jokes.explanation: Solid understated humor. Pam's diplomatic phrasing for Michael's complete work avoidance is characteristically polite but knowing.

jokes.text: But once a year, it all falls on the same Friday. And that's today. I call it the perfect storm.
jokes.setup: Pam listing Michael's avoided responsibilities
jokes.punchline: The dramatic 'perfect storm' metaphor for basic paperwork
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation with the cinematic reference. Pam's dry delivery of the 'perfect storm' metaphor for mundane office tasks works well.

jokes.text: 'I don't want to work,' 'I just want to bang on this mug all day.'
jokes.setup: Michael avoiding paperwork
jokes.punchline: His childish declaration while literally banging on a mug
jokes.explanation: Pure Michael childishness. The visual of him literally banging on a mug while declaring he doesn't want to work is perfectly immature.

jokes.text: Here's the bizzle. I have a very top secret mission for you.
jokes.setup: Michael addressing Ryan formally
jokes.punchline: The made-up slang 'bizzle' followed by dramatic 'top secret mission' language
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael trying-too-hard slang combined with his tendency to dramatize mundane tasks. Moderately funny but relies on Michael's consistent character quirks.

jokes.text: Why is that secret? / Hello. Oh, God. Busy work. Ah, get away, get away. Cretin.
jokes.setup: Ryan questioning the 'secret' nature of updating contacts
jokes.punchline: Michael's over-the-top avoidance of Pam and calling her a 'cretin'
jokes.explanation: Michael's dramatic avoidance of responsibility is in character but the 'cretin' insult feels unnecessarily mean without being particularly clever.

jokes.text: Shalax, Pam. Stop Pam-MSing.
jokes.setup: Pam trying to get Michael to sign documents
jokes.punchline: Michael's made-up 'Shalax' and 'Pam-MSing' wordplay
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay that's perfectly Michael. 'Pam-MSing' is actually quite clever, combining her name with PMS in a way that's juvenile but inventive.

jokes.text: What if there's a tornado, Pam? People's legs are crushed under rubble. Please, would you be so kind as to call my wife? No I can't. Because we don't have any emergency contact information.
jokes.setup: Pam questioning the priority of updating emergency contacts
jokes.punchline: Michael's elaborate tornado scenario to justify busy work
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that shows Michael's tendency to create elaborate justifications for avoiding real work. The specificity of the tornado scenario is absurdly detailed.

jokes.text: Because Pam said it wasn't a priority. Think. Think with your head Pam.
jokes.setup: Continuation of tornado scenario
jokes.punchline: Michael blaming Pam for hypothetical tornado deaths and telling her to 'think with your head'
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that shows Michael's blame-shifting. 'Think with your head' is a perfectly Michael redundant command.

jokes.text: That's the problem with being a boss, is that when you are tough, they resent you, and when you are cool, they walk all over you. / Catch-22. / Catch-22, yes.
jokes.setup: Michael philosophizing about management after Pam leaves
jokes.punchline: His proud agreement with Ryan's 'Catch-22' reference he clearly doesn't understand
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael pretending to understand literary references. His immediate enthusiastic agreement reveals he has no idea what Catch-22 actually means.

jokes.text: This is Michael Jackson, calling from Wonderland. / You mean Neverland? / This is Tito.
jokes.setup: Michael doing prank calls while avoiding work
jokes.punchline: His wrong reference to 'Wonderland' then switching to pretending to be Tito Jackson
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael pop culture confusion. The quick pivot to Tito Jackson shows his improvisational panic but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: Your major and minor lines cross at a ridge. / That sucks. / You're making this up as you go along, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Jim pretending to read Pam's palm
jokes.punchline: Pam calling out his obvious improvisation of fake palm reading
jokes.explanation: Sweet Jim/Pam moment with gentle humor. Pam's direct call-out of Jim's BS is charming and shows their comfortable dynamic.

jokes.text: Well, at least I don't have cavities. / Yes, you have very nice teeth. / Thanks.
jokes.setup: Jim's bad palm reading news
jokes.punchline: Pam's random cavity comment leading to Jim's genuine compliment
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment that's more sweet than funny. Shows their easy rapport but isn't particularly comedic.

jokes.text: This is Mike Tyson.
jokes.setup: Michael continuing prank calls
jokes.punchline: His attempt to impersonate Mike Tyson over the phone
jokes.explanation: Weak prank call humor that relies on random celebrity impersonation. Shows Michael's immaturity but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: As sempai, do you ever think there's gonna be a day, where humans and robots can peacefully coexist? / Impossible. The way they're programmed... / You're mocking me.
jokes.setup: Jim asking Dwight a seemingly serious philosophical question about his karate wisdom
jokes.punchline: Dwight falling for it completely before realizing he's being mocked
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jim prank that plays on Dwight's newly inflated ego as 'sempai'. The absurd question about robots is perfectly calculated to get a serious Dwight response.

jokes.text: That's not advice. What advice sounds like is this. Don't ever bring your purple belt to work, because someone might... steal it.
jokes.setup: Dwight threatening to 'make an example' of Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim's demonstration of real advice while literally stealing Dwight's belt
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that combines verbal wit with physical action. Jim's lesson on what advice actually sounds like while demonstrating it is brilliantly executed.

jokes.text: Okay, give that back to me. / Okay, say please. / No, that is not a toy. / Please. / Please? / Good. And it absolutely is a toy.
jokes.setup: Dwight demanding his belt back
jokes.punchline: Jim treating him like a child then delivering the crushing 'it absolutely is a toy'
jokes.explanation: Devastating putdown that completely deflates Dwight's martial arts pretensions. The childish 'say please' exchange followed by the brutal dismissal is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: This is not a toy. This is a message, for the entire office. So that everyone could see that I am capable of physically dominating them.
jokes.setup: Dwight in talking head explaining his belt
jokes.punchline: His serious explanation of the belt as an intimidation tactic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dwight's delusional self-perception. The idea of a purple belt as office intimidation is perfectly Dwight.

jokes.text: And this is more of a ying yang thing. The Michael is all cursive, Scott all caps. Left brain, right brain. Or duality of man.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his signature style
jokes.punchline: His pretentious pseudo-philosophical explanation for inconsistent handwriting
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael over-intellectualizing something mundane. The escalation from handwriting to 'duality of man' is perfectly absurd and quotable.

jokes.text: No women or children, unless provoked. / Okay, Roy. / Warehouse guy. Doesn't count.
jokes.setup: Dwight listing people he could fight
jokes.punchline: His immediate dismissal of Roy as 'doesn't count' because he's from the warehouse
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dwight's selective criteria for fights. His dismissal of warehouse workers reveals his office hierarchy mindset.

jokes.text: Michael. Could you beat up Michael? / Yeah, I don't think that would happen. / Cause we're friends. / Because I would kick his ass.
jokes.setup: Jim asking if Dwight could fight Michael
jokes.punchline: Dwight's response being interpreted as friendship loyalty when he means he'd win easily
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke that uses character dynamics perfectly. Michael's misinterpretation followed by Dwight's brutal clarification is expertly timed.

jokes.text: So? I've beat up black belts. Yeah. / How'd you know they were black belts? / They told me, after.
jokes.setup: Michael boasting about fighting black belts
jokes.punchline: Jim's question revealing the absurdity, followed by Michael's oblivious answer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deconstruction of Michael's boast. Jim's simple question exposes the logical flaw, and Michael's answer makes it even more absurd. Perfect structure and timing.

jokes.text: I used to run with a very tough crowd, street fighter types. Real, real bad people. I'm just lucky I got out.
jokes.setup: Michael continuing his tough guy narrative
jokes.punchline: The idea of suburban Michael being in a 'real bad' street fighting crew
jokes.explanation: Solid character humor playing on Michael's tendency to fabricate a cooler past. The dramatic 'I'm just lucky I got out' is perfectly over the top.

jokes.text: When you're a jet, your're a jet all the way, right? / You're a jet?
jokes.setup: Michael referencing West Side Story
jokes.punchline: Jim's deadpan question treating it as if Michael is claiming to be an actual Jet
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Michael's theatrical reference. Jim's literal interpretation undermines Michael's attempt at street cred through musical theater.

jokes.text: Oh Michael! Sleeper hold. Bedtime for vato. / Stop it. / You are, the weakest link!
jokes.setup: Michael demonstrating his fighting skills
jokes.punchline: His random collection of fighting movie clichés and game show references
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that shows Michael's pop culture-derived fighting knowledge. The random 'Weakest Link' reference is typical Michael non sequitur.

jokes.text: But sometimes your best friends, start coming in to work late and, start having dentist appointments that aren't dentist appointments.
jokes.setup: Michael talking about friendship in the office
jokes.punchline: His passive-aggressive reference to fake dentist appointments
jokes.explanation: Good insight into Michael's management style and his awareness of employee deception. The specific 'dentist appointments that aren't dentist appointments' is relatable.

jokes.text: Oh, queer! Eye, Queer Eye. That's a good show. Important show.
jokes.setup: Michael using outdated language then realizing it
jokes.punchline: His awkward overcorrection by praising Queer Eye for the Straight Guy
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate language followed by desperate overcorrection. Shows his awareness of political correctness without understanding it.

jokes.text: Well, that would be kind of worthless, because I know a ton of 14 years old girls who could kick his ass. / You know a ton of 14-year-old girls?
jokes.setup: Michael dismissing Dwight's fighting ability
jokes.punchline: Jim's question highlighting how creepy Michael's statement sounds
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to how Michael's casual statements often sound wrong. Jim's timing makes Michael's innocent comment sound inappropriate.

jokes.text: When we rented Armageddon... he cried at the end of it. / No... / That was because it was New Year's Eve, and it started to snow at exactly midnight.
jokes.setup: Michael exposing Dwight's emotional side
jokes.punchline: Dwight's elaborate justification for crying at Armageddon
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing both Michael's betrayal of Dwight's confidence and Dwight's desperate attempt to save face with an overly specific excuse.

jokes.text: 'Oh, Bruce Willis, they're going to leave him on the asteroid.'
jokes.setup: Michael continuing to mock Dwight
jokes.punchline: His impression of Dwight's emotional reaction to the movie
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up that shows Michael's cruel mockery of his friend's vulnerable moment. The specific Bruce Willis reference makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Did I want to harm Michael, the one man I've been hired to protect? No, I did not.
jokes.setup: Dwight reflecting on punching Michael
jokes.punchline: His over-dramatic description of Michael as 'the one man I've been hired to protect'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight melodrama that elevates his job to a noble calling. The bodyguard language is perfectly in character and absurdly overblown.

jokes.text: I come from a long line of fighters. My maternal grandfather, was the toughest guy I ever knew. World War II veteran. Killed 20 men, then spent the rest of the war in an allied prison camp.
jokes.setup: Michael establishing his fighting heritage
jokes.punchline: The implication that his grandfather was imprisoned by the Allies for killing too many people
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy that reveals Michael's complete misunderstanding of war heroics. The casual mention of Allied imprisonment suggests war crimes, which is brilliantly inappropriate.

jokes.text: My father battled blood pressure and obesity all his life. Different kind of fight.
jokes.setup: Michael continuing his family fighting legacy
jokes.punchline: Equating medical conditions with actual fighting
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that shows Michael's attempt to maintain the fighting narrative even when describing health issues. The 'different kind of fight' is perfectly sincere and absurd.

jokes.text: I'll buy you a bag of chips. / Why are you twisting around? / French onion? / Obviously.
jokes.setup: Pam trying to bribe Kevin to ask Michael something
jokes.punchline: Kevin's immediate acceptance and specific chip flavor request
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment that shows Kevin's simple motivations and Pam's desperation. 'Obviously' is a perfect Kevin response to French onion chips.

jokes.text: Nice.
jokes.setup: Pam asking for Dwight to walk her to her car
jokes.punchline: Jim's understated approval of her strategy
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction that shows Jim's appreciation for clever manipulation. Economy of language makes it work.

jokes.text: Dwight can't stop you from being mugged. He's just not tough enough. / He's a purple belt. That's really high. / I could beat up Dwight. That's ridiculous. I can murder him.
jokes.setup: Pam suggesting Dwight could protect her
jokes.punchline: Michael's escalation from 'beat up' to 'murder'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Michael's competitive nature and casual use of extreme language. The progression to 'murder' is perfectly Michael.

jokes.text: Watch out Kelly, might sucker punch you. / I didn't sucker punch you Michael. / No, really? In case you didn't remember, I was defending my honor. Like samouraï.
jokes.setup: Michael complaining about being punched
jokes.punchline: Dwight's serious explanation involving samurai honor
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dwight's pretentious justification for violence. The samurai reference is perfectly Dwight grandiosity.

jokes.text: Okay, fine, tit for tit.
jokes.setup: Michael negotiating a fair fight
jokes.punchline: His malapropism of 'tit for tat'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael malapropism that's mildly funny but not particularly clever. Shows his tendency to misuse idioms.

jokes.text: If we were in a bar, right now, there would be two punches. Me punching you and you hitting the floor. / No, I'd block your first punch, rending ineffective.
jokes.setup: Michael's tough guy scenario
jokes.punchline: Dwight's technical martial arts response with 'rending ineffective'
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between Michael's movie tough talk and Dwight's pseudo-technical language. 'Rending ineffective' is perfectly pretentious Dwight.

jokes.text: You know what? I have an idea, why don't you just leave right now? Why don't you walk away from the room, okay?
jokes.setup: Toby trying to intervene
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate dismissal of HR attempting to do their job
jokes.explanation: Standard Michael/Toby antagonism. Shows Michael's complete disregard for appropriate workplace behavior but isn't particularly inventive.

jokes.text: The Albany branch is working right through lunch to prevent downsizing. But Michael, he decided to extend our lunch by an hour... so that we could all go down to the dojo and watch him fight Dwight.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining the office situation
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Albany's work ethic and Michael's priorities
jokes.explanation: Excellent workplace satire that highlights Michael's misplaced priorities. The juxtaposition between serious business concerns and playground antics is well-crafted.

jokes.text: I recognize that. That is Japanese for California roll. / No it's not. / I think it is, a guy told me about that.
jokes.setup: Michael commenting on Japanese writing at the dojo
jokes.punchline: His confident but completely wrong translation
jokes.explanation: Good Michael ignorance humor. The specific 'California roll' guess is perfectly Michael - confidently wrong about something cultural.

jokes.text: Actually, it's a symbol for eternal discipline. / Wow, that is really interesting.
jokes.setup: Dwight correcting Michael's translation
jokes.punchline: Michael's fake interest in the actual meaning
jokes.explanation: Minor character moment showing Michael's polite disinterest. Works as character development but isn't particularly funny.

jokes.text: After a clean strike to the chest, stomach, or kidneys, I will separate you and award a point. First person to three wins, alright? / Yes, sensei! / Lot of rules. Lot of rules. On the street, we didn't have any rules. Maybe one, no kicks to the groin, home for dinner.
jokes.setup: Formal dojo rules being explained
jokes.punchline: Michael's street fighting 'rules' that make no sense
jokes.explanation: Great Michael logic that completely misunderstands both street fighting and rules. The 'home for dinner' rule is perfectly absurd and quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, purple belt. / Okay, I got it. / No. / I got his pants. / That was my pants. / No points for pants.
jokes.setup: The formal karate match devolving
jokes.punchline: Michael claiming points for pulling down Dwight's pants
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy that shows Michael's complete misunderstanding of martial arts. The sensei's deadpan 'no points for pants' is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Clean technique gentlemen. / Come on. Do that again. Do that again. I dare you.
jokes.setup: The sensei trying to maintain order
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately escalating to playground threats
jokes.explanation: Shows the contrast between martial arts discipline and Michael's street fighting mentality. Decent character work but not exceptional.

jokes.text: One point. Two points. Three points. / I said break. / How many points now? / I win, I win. / No, you didn't. / Eight points, nine points.
jokes.setup: Michael claiming multiple points
jokes.punchline: His escalating point count despite the sensei's protests
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Michael's refusal to follow rules. The arbitrary point counting is childish in a perfectly Michael way.

jokes.text: You talkin' to me?
jokes.setup: Michael after his 'victory'
jokes.punchline: His Taxi Driver reference while looking in a mirror
jokes.explanation: Standard movie reference that shows Michael's tendency to quote films inappropriately. Not particularly clever but in character.

jokes.text: Raging Bull, Pacino.
jokes.setup: Michael making movie references
jokes.punchline: Getting the actor wrong (it's De Niro in Taxi Driver, not Pacino in Raging Bull)
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael getting pop culture references wrong. The confident incorrectness is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Hi, Ryan. This is Saddam Hussein. / Next message. / Hey Ryan, this is your girlfriend. And I'm mad.
jokes.setup: Michael's prank call messages to Ryan
jokes.punchline: The progression from dictator to fake angry girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Mediocre prank call humor that shows Michael's immaturity. The fake girlfriend bit is particularly juvenile.

jokes.text: My emergency contact is Todd Packer. Todd F. Packer. / You know what the F stands for? / Fudge?
jokes.setup: Michael mentioning Todd Packer's middle initial
jokes.punchline: Ryan's innocent guess of 'Fudge' instead of an expletive
jokes.explanation: Good setup that plays on audience knowledge of Todd Packer while showing Ryan's naivety. The innocent 'Fudge' guess is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Just put... The hospital. / Contact number: Just put 911.
jokes.setup: Dwight changing his emergency contact from Michael
jokes.punchline: His progression from 'hospital' to just '911'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Dwight's complete loss of faith in Michael. The progression to just calling 911 directly is perfectly logical for Dwight.

jokes.text: You know what, do yourself a favor. Just leave me as his contact. And I will call the hospital. Cut out the middleman.
jokes.setup: Dwight wanting to change his emergency contact
jokes.punchline: Michael's business-speak approach to emergency medical care
jokes.explanation: Excellent Michael logic that treats emergency response like a business process. 'Cut out the middleman' for medical emergencies is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Well, if you do anything crazy, give me a shout. / Yeah. Alright, I will, see you Monday.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to connect with Ryan about weekend plans
jokes.punchline: Ryan's polite but clearly dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor showing Michael's desperation to be included and Ryan's polite rejection. More sad than funny.

jokes.text: Let's gang bang this thing and go home.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to motivate everyone to finish work
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate phrasing for teamwork
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate language that's genuinely shocking and quotable. His oblivious delivery makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: I have been testing you the entire day. Did you know that? / Of course.
jokes.setup: Michael claiming the fight was a test
jokes.punchline: Dwight's immediate agreement despite being obviously surprised
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamics where Dwight refuses to admit he didn't see through Michael's 'plan'. Both characters' pride prevents honesty.

jokes.text: So, effective immediately, I am promoting you, from assistant to the regional manager to assistant regional manager.
jokes.setup: Michael offering Dwight a promotion
jokes.punchline: The callback to the earlier 'assistant to' vs 'assistant' distinction
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the series' running gag that provides perfect resolution to the episode's conflict. The formal promotion language makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: So I guess this'll just by my office. / No. No. Title change only.
jokes.setup: Dwight assuming promotion comes with physical benefits
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate shutdown of any actual change
jokes.explanation: Great deflation of Dwight's excitement showing that Michael's 'promotion' is completely meaningless. Perfect timing on Michael's rejection.

jokes.text: Three month probationary period. Let's just not tell anybody about this right now. / Just a formality? / Absolutely. But not really.
jokes.setup: Michael adding more conditions to the 'promotion'
jokes.punchline: His contradictory 'Absolutely. But not really' response
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that shows Michael's complete insincerity. The contradictory statement is perfectly Michael double-talk.

jokes.text: I have so much, to learn, from you. / Yes you do. / Thank you. Sensei.
jokes.setup: Dwight's grateful response to his fake promotion
jokes.punchline: His addition of 'Sensei' title for Michael
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the karate theme with Dwight now viewing Michael as a sensei. Shows how completely he's been manipulated.

jokes.text: I told Dwight that there is honor in losing. Which as we all know is completely ridiculous. But, there is, however, honor in making a loser feel better. Which is what I just did for Dwight.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his philosophy in talking head
jokes.punchline: His callous view of consoling Dwight as honorable manipulation
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy that reveals Michael's manipulative nature while he thinks he's being noble. The casual cruelty disguised as wisdom is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: Would I rather be feared or loved? Easy. Both. I want people to be afraid of how much they love me.
jokes.setup: Michael pondering leadership philosophy
jokes.punchline: His impossible solution that makes no logical sense
jokes.explanation: One of the greatest Michael Scott quotes of all time. Perfect encapsulation of his character - the impossible demand that reveals his complete self-delusion. Brilliantly quotable and endlessly rewatchable.

jokes.text: And I think I proved that today at the dojo.
jokes.setup: Michael concluding his leadership thoughts
jokes.punchline: His belief that the dojo incident proved his point about being feared and loved
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Michael's complete misreading of the day's events. His confidence that he proved anything is perfectly delusional.
